summary: DeVault argues that the hacker community's professed commitment to free speech is selectively applied: hate speech is tolerated while discussions of sexual harassment and assault are actively suppressed. He traces how the community's homogeneous old guard developed a pattern of defending comfortable speech while punishing challenging speech that threatens power structures. The post specifically calls out the protection of known sexual predators at hacker events, including organizers who circumvented codes of conduct to allow rapists to attend. He argues that communities should not hold themselves to courtroom standards of evidence when the goal is community safety, not imprisonment, and calls on hackers to listen to victims and demand accountability from leaders.
key_points: The hacker community's free speech absolutism was always unconsciously contingent on speech that didn't challenge the homogeneous majority's comfort
Both progressive speech and hate speech are permitted only within a carefully regulated framework that preserves existing power structures
Sexual harassment and assault are the truly forbidden topics — those who speak about them face swift retaliation while perpetrators are protected

Communities don't need courtroom standards of evidence because they aren't imprisoning anyone — they're keeping spaces safe, and the 99%+ failure rate of the justice system proves that standard is insufficient
DeVault witnessed organizers at HiP Berlin cooperating to circumvent their own code of conduct to allow known rapists to attend an event
money_quotes: There are forbidden topics in the hacker community. One is sternly reprimanded for bringing them up, by their peers, their leaders, and the community at large.
This was a kind of free speech which was unconsciously contingent on being used for speech with which the listener was comfortable.
The free speech absolutists among the old guard, faced with this discomfort, developed a tendency to defend hate speech and demean speech that challenged them.
One must speak as a victim seeking the pity and grace of your superiors to be permitted space to air your grievances.
Men who assault, harass, and even rape women in our spaces, are protected. A culture of silence is enforced.
Our job is not to punish the accused, but rather to keep our communities safe.
What kind of hacker puts their faith in authority?
Most hackers live under the comfortable belief that it 'can't happen here', but it can and it does.
key_insight: The hacker community's celebrated free speech principles are selectively enforced to protect power structures, with the most aggressive censorship reserved for those who speak about sexual harassment and assault.
